summary: Jeff Atwood examines whether the common preference for black text on white background in code editors is actually supported by research. He cites two studies that provide nuanced findings: while black on white is generally most readable, high contrast isn't always better than medium contrast, and the direction of contrast matters significantly. The studies found that darker text on lighter backgrounds consistently outperforms the inverse due to a phenomenon called halation, where light text on dark backgrounds can appear to glow and blur. Atwood concludes that while any syntax highlighting beats plain text, black on white schemes remain the best choice for readability.
key_points: Research shows contrast affects legibility, but high contrast isn't always superior to medium contrast
Halation (text appearing to glow/blur) makes light text on dark backgrounds harder to read
Darker text on lighter backgrounds consistently rated more readable than the inverse in every color combination tested
Any syntax highlighting scheme is preferable to non-colorized plain text
The most readable combination is black text on white background, with strong preference for combinations containing black
money_quotes: From these results, one can say that contrast affects legibility, but unfortunately, it does not seem to be as simple as high contrast being better than low contrast.
When light text is placed on a dark background the text may seem to glow and become blurred; this is referred to as halation, and it may make the text harder to read.
The most readable color combination is black text on white background; overall, there is a stronger preference for any combination containing black.
In every color combination surveyed, the darker text on a lighter background was rated more readable than its inverse.
I'll take any of these schemes over the non-colorized Notepad version, but I feel very strongly that black on white color schemes are the way to go for overall readability.
key_insight: While developers often choose color schemes based on personal preference, research consistently shows that darker text on lighter backgrounds is more readable due to the halation effect that makes light-on-dark text appear blurred.
